steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank must complete a logical conclusion drawn from the data. The passage sets up a paradox: CLPs are shielded from the environment during transmission yet declined with warming, while directly transmitted parasites are exposed yet stayed stable. The conclusion must explain this counterintuitive result.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — as the number of host species involved in a parasite's transmission increases, the parasite is better protected against rising temperatures.: This directly contradicts the data. CLPs use more host species and are shielded, yet they declined more. ✗
  • Choice B — any advantages that the transmission strategy used by three-host CLPs may have conferred did not completely offset the negative effects of other temperature-driven factors on CLP abundance.: This resolves the paradox exactly. CLPs have a transmission advantage because they are typically shielded from the external environment during transmission, but other temperature-driven factors still caused three-host CLP abundance to decrease as sea temperatures rose. ✓
  • Choice C — CLPs primarily transmitted by ingestion were less dependent on host species adversely affected by warming temperatures than were CLPs that use other transmission strategies.: The passage treats CLPs as a single category (three-host parasites transmitted by ingestion); it provides no basis for comparing subtypes of CLPs. ✗
  • Choice D — directly transmitted parasites identified in the study were more likely to use transmission strategies that shield them from warming temperatures than were three-host CLPs.: The passage states the opposite — directly transmitted parasites are exposed to external conditions, while CLPs are shielded. ✗
That matches Choice B — any advantages that the transmission strategy used by three-host CLPs may have conferred did not completely offset the negative effects of other temperature-driven factors on CLP abundance..
